lesson_title: Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.front: What does a C++ compiler do?
cards.back: The compiler reads C++ source code (.cpp files) and translates it into machine language, producing object files (.o or .obj) that the CPU can execute.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.text: The compiler translates {{c1::.cpp}} source files into {{c2::object files}} (with extensions like {{c3::.o}} or {{c4::.obj}}).
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.front: What happens when the compiler encounters a syntax error?
cards.back: The compiler stops compilation and produces an error message indicating where the problem is and what's wrong. You must fix the error before the code will compile.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.text: Each {{c1::.cpp}} file in a project is compiled {{c2::separately}} into its own {{c3::object file}}.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.back: The linker combines all object files (.o/.obj) and libraries into a single executable program. It also resolves cross-file function calls and ensures all referenced functions exist.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.concept: The difference between compiler errors and linker errors
cards.explanation:
Compiler errors occur during compilation of individual .cpp files:
  • Syntax errors
  • Type mismatches
  • Undeclared variables
Linker errors occur when combining object files:
  • Missing function definitions
  • Duplicate definitions
  • Missing libraries
Compiler errors prevent object file creation. Linker errors prevent executable creation.
examples: // Compiler error: int x = 'hello'; // type mismatch
// Linker error: function declared but never defined
// Linker error: main() function not found
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.text: The complete build process is: {{c1::compile}} .cpp files to object files → {{c2::link}} object files and libraries → produce {{c3::executable}}.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.front: What is a library in C++?
cards.back: A library is a package of precompiled code that provides reusable functionality (functions, classes). Libraries save time by providing common functionality you don't have to write yourself.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic libraries?
cards.back: Static libraries (.a/.lib) are copied into your executable at link time. Dynamic libraries (.so/.dll/.dylib) are loaded at runtime and can be shared between programs.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.text: The {{c1::C++ Standard Library}} is a library that comes with every C++ compiler and provides essential functionality like {{c2::iostream}} for input/output.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.question: Why does this cause a linker error?
cards.answer: Linker error: The add function is declared but never defined. The linker can't find the implementation.
cards.explanation: You must provide a definition (implementation) for the add function, either in the same file or in another .cpp file that gets linked.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries

cards.front: Why do C++ projects compile faster on subsequent builds?
cards.back: The build system only recompiles .cpp files that have changed. Unchanged files use their existing object files, saving compilation time.
cards.source: 0.5 - Introduction to the compiler, linker, and libraries
